lecture_topic_summary: March across the periodic table: which elements are structural materials and why. Production tonnages for steel, cement, aluminum, copper, zinc, nickel, magnesium, titanium, and minor metals. Niche applications (Zr fuel cladding, Hf plasma electrodes, Re in turbine blades, Ir for Voyager). Closing: plastics corrosion / environmental degradation, Delrin failure case, exploding toilets.
key_cases_or_anecdotes: Alaska gas pipeline + International Nickel high-Ni steel (insufficient world Ni supply)
Brittle-crack propagation in gas pipelines + crack-stopper rings
Foundry Educational Foundation conference + cope-and-drag joke (MIT senior year anecdote)
Cost-importance epiphany (anonymous junior faculty colleague + Joel Clark article)
SR-71 Blackbird and B-2 bomber titanium skin
Voyager spacecraft iridium-clad plutonium thermoelectric generator
Nicholas Grant creep course: platinum as ideal turbine blade material
Donald Sadoway's doctoral thesis on niobium/tantalum separation
Bob Rose / International Nickel: palladium-as-nickel-replacement anecdote
DuPont Delrin failure: 1958 paper tested 200 solvents but not water; class-action settlements; exploding toilets
tom_factual_slips_flagged: Vanadium etymology: Tom says 'Greek god of vanity, Vanis'; actually Norse goddess Vanadis. Preserved with bracket correction in p30.
Niobus and Tantalus 'twins': Tom himself flags uncertainty. The mythological pair is Niobe and Tantalus, mother and son, not twins. Preserved with editorial note.
course_context_note: Final lecture of the Fall 2013 offering of 3.371. Tom is wrapping up by surveying the periodic table to reinforce the semester's recurring theme — that only a handful of elements function as structural materials at scale, and cost (energy, ore availability, processing) is what determines which. The lecture also includes a scheduling segment for student presentations and several memoir-mode anecdotes.
